avl:balance_factor_exceeded.explanation: The balance factor of a node in an AVL tree is the difference between the height of its left subtree and the height of its right subtree. This value must be -1, 0, or 1. When the balance factor falls outside this range, the tree has become unbalanced and must be rebalanced using rotations.
step_by_step: Step 1: Identify the unbalanced node — WHY: The balance factor exceeds the allowed range of [-1, 0, 1]
Step 2: Determine the rotation case (LL, LR, RR, RL) — WHY: Each imbalance pattern requires a specific rotation sequence
Step 3: Perform the required rotation(s) — WHY: Rotations redistribute nodes to restore balance while preserving BST ordering
Step 4: Update heights of affected nodes — WHY: Heights must be recalculated after structural changes
Step 5: Verify the tree is now balanced — WHY: All nodes must have balance factors in the allowed range
avl:balance_factor_exceeded.detailed_example: Start with AVL tree containing [10, 5, 15]. Insert 3. After insertion, node 10 has left height 2 and right height 1, balance factor = 1 - 2 = -1? Wait — balance factor = left_height - right_height. After inserting 3: 10 has left subtree height 2 (5->3), right subtree height 1 (15), so balance factor = 2 - 1 = 1. That's fine. But insert 1 as well: 10's left height = 3 (5->3->1), right height = 1, balance factor = 2. This requires a right rotation on 10.
avl:balance_factor_exceeded.why_this_matters: Without rebalancing, AVL trees would degrade to linked lists in worst case, giving O(n) instead of O(log n) search time.
avl:balance_factor_exceeded.guiding_question: Why is the balance factor computed as the difference of subtree heights rather than just checking if the tree is 'roughly balanced'?
avl:balance_factor_exceeded.key_rule: balance_factor = height(left_subtree) - height(right_subtree). |balance_factor| ≤ 1 for all nodes.
avl:balance_factor_exceeded.common_mistake: Students often apply the wrong rotation type. Remember: LL case means the imbalance is in the left child's left subtree — this requires a right rotation on the parent.
avl:balance_factor_exceeded.visual_trace: BEFORE: 10(root) -> 5(left) -> 3(left) [balance factor 2 at 10] | AFTER: 5(root) -> 3(left) -> 10(right) [balance factor 0 at 5]
